speaker: interviewer
text: That's a little pause.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I'm just getting set.

speaker: interviewer
text: Little relaxing.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm just getting a new tape in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: What did he say?

speaker: interviewer
text: He's putting a new tape in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, new tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] like something to drink?

speaker: interviewer
text: You're doing great.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh no, we're fine. Because we'll keep going straight away.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm a little bit excited. My heart goes--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to rest a second?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three interview with Helena Snidman. You were telling me about how you didn't lose your faith in God during the war. And it was your husband that kept you--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct. I didn't lose faith in God. And I still believe in God. Something-- I imagine God not as a person, but a bigger power than humanity can-- can bring, that is what is meant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because what-- I repeat it a hundred times to my children. I say, what was I so special? I considered to be a rebel, because I'm a revisionist. You can't slap my face even today, because I'll slap you back.

speaker: interviewee
text: You understand, this was our motto. This was Jabotinsky's word. When somebody slap your right face, you slap back the left face. And I wasn't such a nebbish. That I was such rebel, my father didn't say goodbye to me when he left. And I took [INAUDIBLE] on my mother. I wanted them to come with me to Russia-- that nothing could persuade me to stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that means what was I so better than anybody else, that everybody perished in the war? And I have to tell you, which is very important to my heart, my husband died the 20th of June, on Father's Day. They kept him alive till Father's Day. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we've made a monument that my grandchildren will know. Next to him, I'll be laying to rest when the time comes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in the back of the monument is written out my maiden name, my husband's family. And they perished in the Holocaust. This is one monument what you're going to see on the whole grave cemetery. And the rabbi was praising us for such a good ideas. This was the ideas of my daughters. Because we don't have a monument from our parents, you know. We don't even know where they are.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now we have a monument on the cemetery. We belong to a shul, to the Beth David. And the rabbi was telling everybody when we made the unveiling to go and to read. So it's written like Towja Fiszer, pronounced in Polish letters, [INAUDIBLE] Fiszer, brothers and sisters, and my little boy, and Michael Snidman, mother and father. That's the only monument in the whole cemetery. Because we were thinking that much we can do for our families at least to make the monument conjunction with my husband's monument. OK. I just want to take you back to 1944. Where did you go after your daughter was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came back to Dubno, to my husband's hometown. And he had some property. So we sold some property. And in meantime, to make a living, I was staying on a market. Because stores wasn't-- there were everything demolished to the war. I was staying on a market with a table. And I was selling variety like pins, like caustic to make soda, to make soap, you know, those things that we knew that is needed for the people at that time. Because it was hard to obtain in the regular stores. And my husband used to travel from Dubno, to Lwow, and bring this. And I was selling.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far--

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Dubno-- how far? As I say before, it's maybe 4 hours traveling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dubno to Lwow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? To Lwow and Dubno, yeah. I think it was like 40 kilometers or something, not too far. 3 and 1/2 hours, you know, it depends how do you went. Because it wasn't regular transportation. You had to take a buggy with a horse, rent. And that man which carried on with the buggy and the horse, he took you there. He wait till my husband shopped, and he brought you back. And in Dubno, we sold [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a few houses. And we went to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you stay in Dubno?

speaker: interviewee
text: At his house.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just need to interrupt. Can you just stop the [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: In one of his houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you stop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And OK. We're going [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in Dubno, where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in one of the houses. They were empty. But it wasn't in a good shape, you know? But we made a home there in one of the houses that my-- my husband's parents owned. But they owned a lot of houses. They were well-to-do people in Dubno. Dubno is a smaller town. And they carried on business with fur.

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to make pelts for the army. And the grandfather of my husband was a well-known man, because he used to be-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how you call it in English? He used to give meat to the army, sell meat to the army. So he was well known. And we were well taken in to Dubno when they see my husband. Even his maid, what she raised him, recognized him. And she used to call him Moishele, Moishele. So that was no problem to get situated in Dubno. It's a small town. It's not Warsaw. What year was it when you arrived there?

speaker: interviewer
text: So where were you right before the war ended, when it was still going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right before the war ended, in Samarkand. And then we went to Dubno.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while you were in Samarkand, how did you hear-- what was it like for you when the war ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Russians were there. So we were thinking that we are safe, anything to get away. However, the Russians didn't treat the refugees that good either. You know, we were exception. Because my husband knew Russian, so he could always find work. He could always get around. And he didn't look much Jewish, so a lot of people didn't know that he was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in Samarkand when the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war ended, I was in Samarkand.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why did you decide to go to Dubno from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Back-- we want to go back to Warsaw to see that maybe somebody's alive. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stopped at Dubno on the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- [SOBBING] we went first to Dubno to see maybe somebody's alive. We wanted to unite with the family. But nobody was. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: So my-- my husband's farther [SOBBING] had a-- a job to cut leather. This was telling us a-- a chairwoman in court, she was testifying. Because when you come and you don't have any documents to show that you are Michael Snidman, you need a witness, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have property below Warsaw, too. But I have no papers to show that I am Towja Fiszer's daughter. [SOBBING] So I dropped the idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to Dubno that maybe we could find any family, any brothers, any anybody. But nobody was alive. And this lady, that Czech particular woman, took care of Mr. Snidman, my husband's father.

speaker: interviewee
text: She used to bring him every day to the factory food. And just before the Germans went away, the day the Germans went away, she took the food to him to the factory. And she begged him.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, Mr. Snidman, come with me to my house. I'll hide you. He didn't want to go, because he was sure that he had papers that he works for the government of the German, for the German Army. He used to make pelts, you know? And when she came next day to bring him the food, he was laying dead in the-- in the factory, Mr. Snidman. When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the Germans went away. I wasn't there. She was testifying in a court, you know, that my husband is the son of the Snidman family. So we could get the houses back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any identification with you when you left Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, none whatsoever. And whatever we had, we threw away. Because sometimes we went on that, sometimes we went on that, just to save our lives. We had no identification whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you say in Dubno?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dubno, we stayed like a year and a half, until 1947. This I remember correctly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in 1947--

speaker: interviewer
text: So none of his-- his relatives survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of his relatives survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody survived-- nobody, nobody, nobody. Nobody from my family, nobody. We were 138 people in our family, uncles and cousins, and their two grandchildren like I mentioned before, and two son-in-laws, nobody, nobody. Because I went back to look for them, for somebody. And I couldn't find them and not my husband either. That was the reason we went to Dubno. And we stayed already. Because by the time it went through legal channels in order of getting the houses-- because we needed money to live on and to travel further. And I-- I stayed on a market. And I was selling those variety things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who looked after your daughter while you were working?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a girl, a Polish girl. She looked after her. And then you didn't have to pay them. You just have to feed them. They were hungry, even the Polish girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when you-- you went back to Warsaw in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Went-- went back to Warsaw in 1947. And we-- I went to look everywhere. Everything was smashed up. And from there, we went to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a-- a city. I forgot the name from the city. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we stayed there till Israel become a state. And we applied to go to Israel, which we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wasn't your daughter born, your second daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In between, Rachel was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it hard to go to Israel from Warsaw, or there was no problem getting papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very big problems, but we-- we got-- we prepaid some under the hand. And we went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of problems did you face?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they didn't want very well to let the people out. It was already communist Poland at that time. So they didn't want the people to leave. And there wasn't much less anti-Semitism than it was before. There were very big anti-Semitism. And I didn't want to raise my children in the same atmosphere that I didn't want to stay during the war time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kinds of papers did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had papers to go to Israel. We eventually got it through the Polish government. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to Israel. I stayed there for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe 14, 15 months. And then we came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you stay in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the Ahuza.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was selling my own clothes to live on it with my two children. I brought clothes, suitcases with clothes, a trunk with clothes. And I was going every day. My Rena was that time five years old. She was looking after-- 5 and 1/2 years old. She was looking after Rachel. She took care of Rachel. And I went every day to the bazaar, sell something and buy food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your husband doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband wasn't working. There was no work. The only work it was is to go to the construction. And he wasn't built for it. So we lived on that.

speaker: interviewer
text: During that time, did you read a lot of people in Israel who had come over from Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Sure, a lot of people, a lot of people from Warsaw. They recognized me, too. And I met a lot of [INAUDIBLE], which I used to belong to the same, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, club. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- when you were socializing, was it mostly people that you knew from Warsaw, or it didn't really make a difference?

words.text: it didn't really make a difference?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, I've met people where a man gave me one room without running water, without toilet, that I could stay with my children in Israel, in Haifa, in that place. And it was a very, very hard life. Because at that time Ben-Gurion wasn't sure what he was doing, you know. It was the-- the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were walking on the street of Haifa, you smelled the smoke from the barrels that the Israelis use to roll barrels with oil. And they were thinking that this was ammunition. You know? You could smell, you could see them. So where did you go from Haifa?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Haifa, I came back to Austria, to Salzburg. There was a lager. Because I had a friend, she passed away. She wrote me that if I take that road, I'll be able to emigrate to the United States. Because my husband was still [INAUDIBLE] a student. And he wasn't built. He was a short man and a skinny. He wasn't built to-- to do that construction work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my friend-- she already passed away. She got in touch with me. And she says if you take that road, so I smuggle-- I paid for it, good money.

speaker: interviewee
text: I flew into Rome with the two children and my husband. And I smuggled the border to Austria, which is bordering to Salzburg. And in Salzburg, we signed ourselves in to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had trouble getting over?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't catch us. They were smuggling a lot of people, not only us. And you paid for it. You paid. It was special smugglers. Even Jewish people, they were smuggling refugees. And in Salzburg, we signed ourselves in to go, or Australia, or America, or Canada, which came first. That's what we took it. And they came, Canada first. So we packed on that little belonging, what we had. And with a ship, we came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Throughout your-- the war and even-- even after, you had to-- the money that you had, do you think that was a big part of what saved you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is?

speaker: interviewer
text: The money that you had, do you think that was a big part of what saved you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, what my father gave me. Now, he didn't give me that much money. Like, he gave me diamonds, jewelry. And I was selling them. I had them hidden very well in a-- a hand mirror, a silver hand mirror from home. I have the hand mirror here. It was packed away with cotton button, you know, rolled in. And it was put a tin. And when they came to check me, that mirror was laying in front. They didn't know that's it's a valuable mirror. Otherwise, they would take it away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your husband hid-- hide money as well, or valuables?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hid at most in the children's clothes, you know. My daughter, my older daughter, had a coat. And it was sewn in in the coat. And when we came to Canada, we didn't know the language. Are you that far, or I'm jumping?

speaker: interviewer
text: I was just going to ask you if it was hard for you to get papers to come to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. To Canada, no. No, it wasn't hard. When it came, our line, we got to Canada. And my husband came as a furrier. He wasn't a furrier. He was working here on any job. And he went to a factory to learn to cut leather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you asked what your religion was when you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, I ran out of tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, he's all right, proceed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me, when you were entering Canada, did you have to show a religion on any of the documents?

speaker: interviewee
text: The what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to write down that you were Jewish when you were entering Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Well, the papers we came with me from Salzburg. And we wasn't hiding that we're Jewish. But my husband came as a furrier, because he had a little idea from his parent's factory. They had a factory from-- pelts, like I mentioned a few times before. So he had a little idea. So he went to a factory to work, to cut leather. But he didn't know how to cut leather.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was standing a Greek man. And my husband didn't even know how the knife looks. So he phones me, go and buy me a knife. So he asked that Greek man to show him how the knife should look. And he taught him how to cut leather. Where was this when he was--

speaker: interviewee
text: In a factory on Spadina, in Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, in Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And when we came to Canada, I worked on two jobs. The Jewish Vocational Service gave me that job. One job was in a factory to fold shirts. And one job was on Queen Street to sell dresses that I could use my languages, which is Russian, Polish. A little bit German I understand. I can go by. And Yiddish, so Yiddish is a little bit German. I worked on those two jobs. And my husband went to learn to cut leather.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Canada the 8th of August-- 7? 7? [NON-ENGLISH] I think the 8th of August, 1951. So the first thing first, I said to my husband, Michael, if this is our home that we're going to live, we have to go and learn English.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went. After work, we went to night courses. But we didn't get any big education, just to go by, you know, that we could know to live by it. Because our timing wasn't to go for any big education. We had to make a living, because we had two children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my daughter-- I-- I lived in a Congress house on Phoebe. The Jewish Congress owned this house. So we lived there. And my daughter was going to a school. So she was helping the nurse [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with Polish language to translate from English.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived in Canada, did you encounter anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not exactly. But that-- that is-- they're speaking out even in-- in the store. In this store now-- at that time, we-- we did. It's a matter of fact that I have a book here on the library written by Professor Abella, which is now the president of the Jewish Congress, that That means it was big anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's stop here.
